topic: Designing self-correcting agentic AI to mitigate LLM hallucinations in academic literature reviews.
final_review:
The development of self-correcting agentic AI is crucial in mitigating Large Language Model (LLM) hallucinations in academic literature reviews (Dwivedi et al., 2023). LLM hallucinations refer to the generation of false or misleading information by AI models, which can have significant consequences in academic research (Kasneci et al., 2023). To address this issue, researchers have emphasized the need for explainable AI (XAI) that can provide transparent and interpretable results (Arrieta et al., 2019). XAI can help identify and correct errors in LLMs, thereby improving the accuracy and reliability of academic literature reviews.

The use of LLMs in academic research has been increasingly popular, with applications in education (Zawacki-Richter et al., 2019) and healthcare (Sallam, 2023). However, the potential risks and challenges associated with LLMs, such as hallucinations and biases, must be carefully considered (Dwivedi et al., 2023). To mitigate these risks, researchers have proposed the development of ethical frameworks for AI development and deployment (Floridi et al., 2018).

Recent studies have explored the use of generative AI models in academic research (Rudolph et al., 2023). While these models have shown promise, they are not without limitations and challenges (Dwivedi et al., 2023). The development of self-correcting agentic AI is essential to detect and correct errors in LLMs, ensuring the accuracy and reliability of academic literature reviews. Researchers have investigated the applications of LLMs in various fields, including education and healthcare, and have highlighted the need for transparent and interpretable AI models (Arrieta et al., 2019; Zawacki-Richter et al., 2019; Sallam, 2023).

References:
Arrieta, A. B., Díaz-Rodríguez, N., & Del Ser, J. (2019)
Dwivedi, Y. K., Kshetri, N., & Hughes, L. (2023)
Floridi, L., Cowls, J., & Beltrametti, M. (2018)
Kasneci, E., Seßler, K., & Küchemann, S. (2023)
Rudolph, J., Tan, S., & Tan, S. (2023)
Sallam, M. (2023)
Zawacki-Richter, O., Marín, V. I., & Bond, M. (2019)
